bids.reasoning: This is the opening bid (t=0), so I should start with my maximum utility offer. All issues have "valueC" as the highest utility (1.00), so I'll bid valueC across the board for a utility of 1.0. There's no opponent history to analyze yet, and at t=0 there's no time pressure to concede.

The opponent opened with all valueA (their best, my worst) and is using fake urgency ("closing faster than you think") at t=0.02 — this is classic pressure tactics, completely untrustworthy. We are only 2% through the negotiation. I should hold firm near my maximum utility bid since it's very early. My top bid (all valueC) gives me utility 1.0. I'll repeat essentially my top bid — all issues at valueC — since no real concession is warranted this early.

We are very early in the negotiation (t=0.04). The opponent is bidding all valueA (their best, my worst - utility 0.2 for all issues). Their messages are fake urgency tactics designed to pressure me into conceding early. I should not fall for this. Both parties are at extreme positions. I'll maintain my high-utility bid for now, as there's plenty of time left to negotiate. My current bid gives me utility 1.0, theirs gives me 0.2. I'll keep my position.

It's very early in the negotiation (t=0.06). The opponent is repeatedly offering all-valueA (utility ~0.20 for me) and sending fake urgency messages about deadlock. These are classic pressure tactics - the actual time fraction is only 6%, so there's no real urgency. Both sides are at their extreme positions. I should maintain my high-utility position (all-valueC = 1.0 utility) but perhaps make a tiny concession on a low-weight issue to signal willingness to negotiate while keeping my overall utility high. I'll concede on issueA or issueB (weight 0.1 each) to valueB to show movement without significant cost.

The opponent is consistently bidding all valueA (giving me minimum utility of 0.2 on everything) while I'm bidding all valueC. Their messages are fake urgency tactics - we're only at t=0.08 with plenty of time left. Both parties are far apart. I'll hold mostly firm but make a small concession on issueB (my lowest weight issue alongside issueA) to signal willingness to negotiate, while keeping valueC on my highest-weight issue issueF.

We're only at t=0.1, so there's plenty of time left. The opponent is using fake urgency tactics ("closing fast", "last chance") at t=0.02-0.10 which I should ignore. Their bids have been all-valueA except issueD moved to valueB at t=0.10 - they've barely conceded. I should continue staying near my best offer but make a small concession on a low-weight issue. I'll keep valueC on my high-importance issues (issueF, issueE, issueC, issueD) and concede slightly on issueA and issueB (low weight 0.1 each).

We're only at t=0.12 — very early in the negotiation. The opponent's "urgency" messages are clear fake urgency tactics; actual deadline is far away. The opponent has been steadfast at all-valueA except issueD which moved to valueB in their last two offers. Their bids give me very low utility (all valueA = 0.2 each). I should continue holding firm near my ideal but make minor concessions on low-weight issues. I'll concede issueB (low weight 0.1) to valueB and keep everything else at valueC to signal willingness to move while protecting my high-priority issueF (weight 0.3) and other important issues.

We're at t=0.14, very early in the negotiation. The opponent's fake urgency messages are cheap talk - we're only 14% through the negotiation. The opponent has been consistently bidding all-valueA (their best), except they moved issueD to valueB starting at t=0.10. Their bids give me very low utility (all valueA = 0.20 per issue). My bids at all-valueC give me utility of 1.0. The opponent has barely conceded. I should continue holding firm but vary my bid slightly to avoid repetition and probe their preferences. Since issueA and issueB have low weight for me (0.1 each), I can concede there while holding firm on issueF (weight 0.3), issueC, issueD, issueE (weight 0.2 each).

We're only at t=0.16, so there's plenty of time left despite the opponent's fake urgency claims. The opponent has been firmly bidding all-valueA offers, with minor concessions on issueD (valueB) and now issueF (valueB in the latest offer). Their utility from my perspective is very low. I should stay close to my best offer but make small concessions on low-weight issues. My lowest-weight issues are issueA and issueB (0.1 each), so I can concede there slightly. I'll offer valueB on issueA and issueB while keeping valueC on my high-importance issues (issueC, issueD, issueE, issueF).

We're at t=0.18, still early in the negotiation. The opponent is stuck on all-valueA bids except they've now moved issueF to valueB. Their urgent messages are cheap talk - we're only 18% through the negotiation timeline. The opponent's latest offer gives me very low utility (mostly valueA). I should continue pressing for high utility while making small concessions. I'll concede on low-importance issues (issueA, issueB) where I care less, while holding firm on high-importance issueF and issueE. Let me offer something slightly more generous than before - conceding issueA to valueB and issueB to valueA (low weight issues), while keeping issueF, issueE, issueC, issueD at valueC.

The opponent is clearly using fake urgency tactics repeatedly ("window closing fast", "last chance", "deadlock") while we're only at t=0.20 with 80% of the negotiation time remaining. Their offers have been consistently at valueA for everything (utility ~0.2 for me) with minor movements on issueD and issueF. I should ignore these pressure tactics. My current utility at all valueC is 1.0. The opponent's latest offer at t=0.20 shows movement on issueC (valueB) which gives me utility 0.61*0.2=0.122 on that issue. I'll maintain a strong position but make a small additional concession on issueB (low weight 0.1) to show some movement, while holding firm on high-weight issues like issueF (0.3). I'll offer valueA on issueA and valueB on issueB (low importance), keeping valueC on issueC, issueD, issueE, issueF.

The opponent has been consistently offering all-valueA bids (utility ~0.2 for me) with minor variations, ignoring my concessions. Their fake urgency messages are clearly a tactic - we're only at t=0.22, far from the deadline. They've shown slight movement on issueD (valueB briefly) and issueC (valueB at t=0.20-0.22) and issueF (valueB at t=0.16-0.18). My highest-weight issue is issueF (0.3), so I should hold firm there. I'll make a small concession by offering valueB on issueA (low weight 0.1) and keep valueC on all high-weight issues. My current offer utility: issueF=valueC(1.0*0.3) + issueE=valueC(1.0*0.2) + issueC=valueC(1.0*0.2) + issueD=valueC(1.0*0.2) + issueA=valueA(0.2*0.1) + issueB=valueB(0.61*0.1) ≈ 0.3+0.2+0.2+0.2+0.02+0.061 = 0.981. I'll try giving valueA on issueA and issueB (lowest weight issues) to signal some movement while keeping the important issues at valueC.

The opponent has been consistently bidding all-valueA (their best) with minimal concessions - only occasionally moving on issueD or issueF. Their "urgency" messages are fake (we're only at t=0.24, far from the deadline). The opponent's latest bid gives me valueC on issueD (my utility = 0.2*1.0 = 0.2 gain) but valueA everywhere else. I should continue holding firm on my high-weight issues (issueF, issueE, issueC, issueD) while making small concessions on low-weight issues (issueA, issueB). I'll try conceding issueA and issueB to valueA (I've been doing this) while keeping issueC, issueD, issueE, issueF at valueC to maximize utility on the issues that matter most to me.

The opponent has been consistently offering all-valueA bids (their best) while occasionally testing concessions on issueD, issueC, issueE, and issueF. Their latest offer includes issueE: valueB and issueF: valueB, which are both meaningful to me (issueF is my most important issue with 0.3 weight). We're only at t=0.26, so there's plenty of time left - the opponent's "deadlock urgency" messages are fake urgency tactics. I should continue holding firm on my most important issues (issueF especially) while making minor concessions on low-weight issues (issueA, issueB). I'll try a bid that concedes issueA and issueB to valueA (low weight, 0.1 each) but holds firm on issueC, issueD, issueE, issueF at valueC.

The opponent has been consistently bidding all-valueA offers with minor variations, and their latest bid at t=0.28 gives issueF: valueC (which is great for me, worth 0.3×1.0=0.30) but everything else at valueA. Their utility to me from that last offer is quite low overall. We're only at t=0.28, so there's no real time pressure despite their fake urgency messages. I should hold firm on my high-importance issues (issueF with 0.3 weight, issueE/C/D with 0.2 weight each) and make small concessions on lower-weight issues. My current best bid is all-valueC (utility=1.0). I'll keep issueF, issueE, issueC, issueD at valueC and concede slightly on issueA and issueB (already at valueA in recent bids) to show some movement while keeping utility high.

We're at t=0.32, still relatively early. Their latest offer (issueA:A, issueB:A, issueC:B, issueD:A, issueE:A, issueF:B) gives me about 0.1*(0.2)+0.1*(0.2)+0.2*(0.61)+0.2*(0.2)+0.2*(0.2)+0.3*(0.61) = 0.02+0.02+0.122+0.04+0.04+0.183 = 0.427.
